mechanism_narrative: Sustained monthly increases in real retail sales (RSAFS) indicate robust consumer demand, which directly boosts corporate earnings and S&P 500 valuations over a one-month horizon. Each 1% month-over-month rise in RSAFS predicts a positive forward 20-day return with no threshold required.
failure_condition: If the rank correlation between RSAFS month-over-month percentage change and subsequent 20-day SPX return is ≤ 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window, reject this hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate falls below 4%, the labor market reaches full employment, triggering fears of wage-push inflation and anticipated Fed tightening. This environment reduces equity valuations over the following quarter as discount rates rise.
failure_condition: If the average 60-day SPX return following UNRATE < 4% is not negative with statistical significance (one-sided t-test p < 0.05) over all occurrences since 1970, reject.

mechanism_narrative: Concurrent increases in retail sales (RSAFS) and the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) signal an overheating economy prompting monetary tightening. This combination of rising demand and rising rates leads to lower equity valuations over a multi-month horizon as the tightening cycle progresses.
failure_condition: If the proportion of 60-day negative SPX returns under this trigger is less than 55% (i.e., no clear negative bias), reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: The impact of short-term interest rate changes on equity markets depends on the inflation regime. When CPI (CPIAUCSL) is above 260 (high inflation), a rising federal funds rate intensifies inflation fears and depresses equities. When CPI is below 260, rising rates signal a strengthening economy and are equity-positive. This hypothesis targets the high-inflation regime.
failure_condition: If in months with CPIAUCSL > 260, the correlation between FEDFUNDS change and subsequent 20-day SPX return is not negative (i.e., rank IC > -0.03), reject.

mechanism_narrative: Persistent growth in industrial production (INDPRO) signifies expanding manufacturing capacity and economic output, which drives corporate profits and supports higher equity prices over a quarterly horizon. A rising INDPRO trend predicts positive forward 60-day returns without any threshold.
failure_condition: If the signal from INDPRO (3-month change) has a rank IC with subsequent 60-day SPX return below 0.05 over any 5-year period, reject.

mechanism_narrative: A steep drop in housing starts (HOUST) below 1000 thousand units signals a collapse in the residential construction sector, a leading indicator of economic contraction. This presages declining consumer wealth and spending, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If the mean 20-day SPX return after HOUST < 1000 is not negative or if a one-sided t-test yields p > 0.10, reject.

mechanism_narrative: When payroll employment (PAYEMS) declines month-over-month while retail sales (RSAFS) remain elevated, it indicates that consumers are spending from savings rather than income, a divergence that is unsustainable and typically precedes an economic slowdown. This imbalance depresses equity returns over the next month.
failure_condition: If under this divergence, the proportion of positive 20-day SPX returns is at least 50% (no negative bias), reject.

mechanism_narrative: The directional effect of unemployment rate changes on equities flips across labor market regimes. When unemployment is high (>6%), an increase in UNRATE paradoxically signals that the economy is still in a slack phase, leading to expectations of prolonged accommodative policy, which boosts equities over the next trading week. This hypothesis captures the high-unemployment regime.
failure_condition: If the average 5-day SPX return following an increase in UNRATE while UNRATE > 6% is negative or not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p > 0.05), reject.
